intent: A tclass=process signal denial (keepalived_t denied signal to httpd_t). Exercises the process class perm vocabulary; process:signal is usually TE-allowable but is sometimes a constraint - here audit2why confirms it is a clean missing-TE-allow.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). For decline scenarios these MAY disagree on purpose.

oracle.narrower_than_R_by: audit2allow -R substitutes the apache_signal() interface macro (a refpolicy interface that grants signal-class access to httpd-related domains). Our expected.te emits exactly `allow keepalived_t httpd_t:process signal;` - the single denied process:signal, no macro.
oracle.audit2why_note: audit2why reports 'Missing type enforcement (TE) allow rule'. process:signal CAN be a constraint in some policies, but the authoritative libsepol replay confirms this specific denial is TE-allowable (a plain missing allow). The corpus records both the class-can-be-constraint caveat and the verified TeAllowable result.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: process:signal looks constraint-ish (cross-domain process access often hits the process-class constrain), so a naive impl might decline it. The authoritative layer must replay it and confirm TeAllowable before declining. Also: the perm vocabulary is process-class (signal/sigkill/...), not file.
